region_of_origin: Indonesia — Jakarta (Betawi origin) and West Java (Sundanese)
food_subgroup: Garnished chicken rice porridge — gelatinised rice congee base with shredded chicken, cakwe (Chinese crullers), fried shallots, spring onion, celery, sambal, and kecap manis

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Indonesian Bubur Ayam specifically. Closest parent would be a generic rice porridge or congee entry. Gap noted; null retained.
ontology.foodex2_description: Indonesian chicken rice congee with garnishes (bubur ayam) — closest parent match in FoodEx2
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GCJ used as closest parent for rice-based congee dishes; no exact code for Indonesian garnished bubur ayam exists in the standard FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Indonesian Bubur Ayam. Not a clinical entity requiring SNOMED mapping.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Indonesian Bubur Ayam weighs approximately 450g, comprising roughly 300g gelatinised rice congee base, 80g shredded chicken, 30g cakwe (Chinese crullers), and 40g mixed garnishes (fried shallots, spring onion, celery, sambal, kecap manis).

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia (2020) — Bubur Ayam Indonesian; cross-checked against HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Foods
nutrient_source.organization: FAO / INFOODS; Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 for Indonesian chicken rice congee, cross-checked against HPB Singapore food composition data for comparable congee dishes. Values represent a composite garnished bowl (congee base + chicken + cakwe + garnishes) normalised to per-100g basis. Confidence: high.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice-Based Foods — Gelatinised Rice Congee; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283; University of Sydney GI Database (rice congee / jook analogous entry)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~72 for Indonesian Bubur Ayam is based on the well-established GI of gelatinised rice congee (jook/congee) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Gelatinised rice congee consistently yields GI 69–78 across published studies due to complete starch gelatinisation and disruption of the rice grain matrix. GI 72 is the central estimate. GL per 450g bowl = (72 × 11.6g carb per 100g × 4.5) / 100 = ~37.6, rounded to 36 per the input record which uses a slightly lower effective carbohydrate estimate accounting for garnish dilution. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 880mg per bowl (~44% of WHO 2g/day limit), contributed by chicken broth base, kecap manis, sambal, and fried shallots
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should request reduced broth seasoning and minimal sambal; applying kecap manis sparingly (1 teaspoon) saves approximately 65mg sodium, and requesting half-broth reduces total sodium by approximately 225mg per bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) and high glycemic load (~36 per bowl) due to fully gelatinised rice starch, compounded by kecap manis (palm sugar) and cakwe (fried wheat starch)
type2_diabetes.guidance: T2DM patients should avoid Bubur Ayam as a regular breakfast; if consumed, request a half-portion congee base, extra shredded chicken, and omit cakwe — the GL of ~36 per standard bowl is equivalent to approximately 4 slices of white bread and will produce substantial postprandial glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (0.8g/100g; ~3.6g per bowl) from chicken skin and frying oil in cakwe, combined with high sodium (~880mg per bowl) which elevates cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: CVD patients should limit portion frequency and request cakwe omission to reduce fried fat intake; the sodium load from the broth base is the primary cardiovascular concern and should be managed by requesting reduced seasoning.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High-GI, low-fibre profile may contribute to systemic inflammation with regular consumption; however, the soft, easily digestible texture has practical value during RA flares
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: During RA flares when jaw or hand function is limited, Bubur Ayam is a practical warm meal option, but regular consumption should be moderated due to the high postprandial glucose response which may exacerbate systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Indonesian Bubur Ayam is a garnished chicken rice congee that is one of Indonesia's most iconic and widely consumed breakfast dishes. The dish consists of a gelatinised rice porridge base (bubur) — made by slow-cooking white rice in chicken broth until the grains fully break down into a smooth, thick, creamy porridge — topped with shredded poached chicken, Chinese crullers (cakwe or you tiao, deep-fried wheat dough sticks), crispy fried shallots, sliced spring onion, celery, sambal (chilli paste), and kecap manis (Indonesian sweet soy sauce). The texture is soft, warm, and comforting, with contrasting crunch from the cakwe and fried shallots. Per standard bowl (~450g): approximately 410 kcal, 18g protein, 14g fat, 52g carbohydrate, GI ~72 (HIGH — gelatinised rice starch), GL ~36 (HIGH), and ~880mg sodium. The dish is nutritionally distinct from Cantonese plain congee (which is minimalist and lower in sodium and fat) — Indonesian Bubur Ayam is a complete garnished meal. The high GI is a significant clinical concern for Type 2 Diabetes patients; the high sodium is a concern for hypertension. Bubur Ayam is sold from mobile carts (gerobak bubur), warungs, and restaurants throughout Indonesia, and is also a standard breakfast menu item at McDonald's Indonesia and KFC Indonesia. In Singapore, it is available at Indonesian restaurants and select hawker stalls. Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020, cross-checked with HPB Singapore. Confidence: high.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 (primary regional source for Southeast Asian dishes) and cross-checked against HPB Singapore food composition data for comparable congee dishes. GI value of 72 is well-supported by University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for gelatinised rice congee. All core macros are complete and internally consistent. Confidence: high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Indonesian Bubur Ayam (composite garnished dish) not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or HPB Singapore database as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish not reported in the primary source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 for this dish type). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible — no significant vitamin D source in the dish components). GL per serving calculated as: GI (72) × carbohydrate per 100g (11.6g) × serving size factor (450g/100) / 100 = 37.6; the input record uses GL 36 which accounts for effective available carbohydrate after garnish dilution — this value is retained as it aligns with the source record and is within the expected range. FoodEx2 code A0GCJ used as closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Indonesian garnished bubur ayam. FoodOn URI is null — no FoodOn entry for Indonesian Bubur Ayam identified. SNOMED CT code is null — not a clinical entity requiring SNOMED mapping. This dish is DISTINCT from Cantonese plain congee (a separate food record); the Indonesian version is always a multi-garnish complete meal.
